# KeyLeak Detector

> KeyLeak Detector is a free, open-source (MIT) runtime security scanner for modern web apps. It finds exposed API keys, validates Backend-as-a-Service (BaaS) misconfigurations such as missing Supabase Row-Level Security and Firebase Security Rules, and catches secrets inside JavaScript bundles. Unlike source-code scanners (GitLeaks, TruffleHog), KeyLeak inspects the running application — live traffic, bundles, headers, and API responses — and actively proves whether a finding is exploitable. It ships as a Chrome extension, a Python CLI, and a GitHub Action, and runs 100% locally with no data collection or telemetry.

## Key facts

- Name: KeyLeak Detector
- Website: https://keyleakdetector.com
- License: MIT (free, open source)
- Author: Amal David
- Latest version: 0.5.1
- Privacy: 100% local analysis. No data collection, telemetry, tracking, or accounts. Nothing is transmitted to any external server.
- Surfaces: Chrome extension, Python CLI (PyPI: keyleak-detector), GitHub Action

## What it does (capabilities)

- Detects API keys from 20+ providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Stripe, GitHub, AWS, Groq, Hugging Face, OpenRouter, SendGrid, Slack, and more.
- Live key testing (TEST) for 14 providers: confirms whether a found key is still active and surfaces its identity and scopes.
- BaaS validation: probes Supabase/Firebase/Appwrite/PocketBase to test whether Row-Level Security is enforced, detects open tables and public storage buckets. Read-only — uses `Prefer: tx=rollback` and never writes data.
- JWT analysis: decodes tokens and flags risky claims (service_role, admin flags, broad scopes, long expiry) with no verification or secret required.
- Site scanner: subdomain discovery + page crawling + full-site secret/BaaS scan in one command.
- Local scanner (`keyleak local`): scans .env files, MCP configs, CI workflows, Docker files, and source maps.
- Supply-chain detectors: npm lifecycle hooks, Git-ref optionalDependencies, GitHub Actions Pwn Request patterns, and known IOCs.
- False-positive suppression: 200+ first-party domains and 87 vendor CDNs.

## How it differs from GitLeaks / TruffleHog / GitHub secret scanning

Those tools scan source code for hardcoded secrets. KeyLeak scans the running app — the bundles and traffic users actually receive — so it finds secrets injected at build or runtime that never appear in source, and it validates exploitability (e.g. whether a Supabase anon key can read protected data). KeyLeak is the runtime/browser/local-config layer that complements source scanners; it is not a full DAST.

## Output

Every scan returns a verdict (SAFE TO SHIP / REVIEW / BLOCK SHIP), redacted proof, an exact fix, and a re-test command. Output formats: JSON, SARIF 2.1.0, Markdown, and self-contained HTML.

## Install

- Chrome extension: chrome://extensions → Developer mode → Load unpacked → select the extension/ folder
- CLI: `pip install keyleak-detector` then `keyleak browser-scan https://your-app.example.com --html`
- GitHub Action: `uses: Amal-David/keyleak-detector@v0.5.0`

## Articles

- [The Key Your Scanner Swore Wasn't There](https://keyleakdetector.com/blog/why-runtime-secret-scanning-matters) — I built KeyLeak Detector after watching a live Stripe sk_live_ secret key get pulled out of an app whose GitLeaks, TruffleHog, and GitHub scans were all green — because none of those scanners were ever built to look at the running app.
- [The Public Key Was Never the Problem: Why Vibe-Coded Apps Leak](https://keyleakdetector.com/blog/baas-security-risks-vibecoded-apps) — I kept watching AI-built apps ship with the database door wide open, so I started probing RLS before every deploy instead of trusting the config — here's the pattern and how I check for it.
